Create a Blazor Web App for building inspectors on construction sites.

Requirements:
- Inspectors fill out safety checklists and add notes at each site visit
- Photos are attached to inspection reports
- Construction sites frequently have poor or no cellular connectivity — the app must continue working offline
- When connectivity returns, data syncs back to the server
- Inspectors use phones and tablets, not desktops
- Low-latency UI is critical — inspectors tap through checklist items quickly
- No authentication needed in the initial version

# Create a Blazor Web App

## Before You Start — Gather Requirements

If the user's request doesn't make the following clear, ask before scaffolding:

1. **What does the app do?** List the main screens/features (e.g., "product catalog with search and shopping cart").
2. **What kind of interactivity is needed?** Displaying data and forms? Real-time updates? Offline support? Rich drag-and-drop UI?
3. **Deployment environment?** Internet-facing? Intranet? Mobile users on slow connections?
4. **Authentication needed?** Anonymous? Individual accounts? Organizational (Azure AD)?

## Pick the Right Interactivity Level

Blazor render modes are a progression scale. Start at the simplest level that satisfies the requirements and only move up when there's a concrete reason.

```
Static SSR ──→ SSR + Enhanced Nav ──→ Interactive Server ──→ Interactive WebAssembly
 simplest                                                           most complex
```

### Decision Rules

| If the app needs... | Use | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Display data, simple forms, links between pages | **Static SSR** (`-int None`) | No JS runtime, no circuit, no WebAssembly download. Forms work via HTML POST. Enhanced navigation makes it feel snappy. |
| Everything above + a few components with client-side behavior (live search, real-time updates, complex form wizards) | **Interactive Server, per-page** (`-int Server`) | Only the components that need interactivity opt in with `@rendermode`. The rest stays static. Server-side execution, full .NET access, no API layer needed. |
| Most pages need rich interactivity (dashboards, drag-and-drop, chat) | **Interactive Server, global** (`-int Server -ai`) | Every component is interactive by default. Consistent UX, simpler mental model. Trade-off: every user holds a SignalR circuit on the server. |
| Network latency is a problem, users are on mobile/poor connections, or the app must work offline | **Interactive WebAssembly** (`-int WebAssembly`) | Code runs in the browser. Eliminates round-trip latency but requires a `.Client` project, API layer for data access, and downloads the .NET runtime to the browser on first visit. For offline support, enable PWA: add a service worker and manifest after scaffolding (not included in the template by default). |
| Fast initial load (Server) + low latency after (WebAssembly) | **Interactive Auto** (`-int Auto`) | First visit uses Server; subsequent visits use cached WebAssembly runtime. Most complex setup — see Auto constraints below. Only choose when both Server and WebAssembly constraints apply. |

**Default recommendation:** Start with `-int Server` (per-page). It covers the vast majority of apps. Upgrade to global or WebAssembly only when a specific requirement demands it.

### Auto Mode Constraints

Auto mode means your component code runs on the server first, then in the browser on subsequent visits. This creates real constraints:

- **All interactive components must live in the `.Client` project** — same as WebAssembly.
- **No direct server access** from interactive components — no EF `DbContext`, no file system, no server-only services. All data access must go through HTTP APIs.
- **Both `Program.cs` files must register matching services** — the server and client DI containers must both provide implementations for any service an interactive component injects.
- **Code must not assume its execution environment** — no `HttpContext` access, no browser-only APIs without `RendererInfo` guards.
- **Test in both modes** — a component that works on Server during development may break on WebAssembly in production (second visit). Test both paths.

### Don'ts

- Don't pick WebAssembly "because it's cool" — it adds a `.Client` project, forces API-mediated data access, and downloads ~10MB to the browser on first visit.
- Don't pick Auto unless you can articulate why Server alone and WebAssembly alone are both insufficient.
- Don't pick global interactivity for apps where most pages are read-only content — per-page keeps the static pages fast and reduces server memory.

## Scaffold the Project

### Static SSR Only (display data + simple forms)

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int None
```

No interactive runtime. Enhanced navigation enabled by default via `blazor.web.js`.

### Interactive Server, Per-Page (recommended default)

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int Server
```

Pages are static by default. Add `@rendermode InteractiveServer` to components that need interactivity.

### Interactive Server, Global

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int Server -ai
```

All pages interactive via `<Routes @rendermode="InteractiveServer" />` in `App.razor`.

### Interactive WebAssembly, Per-Page

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int WebAssembly
```

Creates `{AppName}` (server) and `{AppName}.Client` (WebAssembly) projects. Interactive components must live in `.Client`.

### Interactive WebAssembly, Global

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int WebAssembly -ai
```

### Interactive Auto, Per-Page

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int Auto
```

### Interactive Auto, Global

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int Auto -ai
```

### With Authentication

Append `-au Individual` to any command above:

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int Server -au Individual
```

# Create a Blazor Web App

## Before You Start — Gather Requirements

If the user's request doesn't make the following clear, ask before scaffolding:

1. **What does the app do?** List the main screens/features (e.g., "product catalog with search and shopping cart").
2. **What kind of interactivity is needed?** Displaying data and forms? Real-time updates? Offline support? Rich drag-and-drop UI?
3. **Deployment environment?** Internet-facing? Intranet? Mobile users on slow connections?
4. **Authentication needed?** Anonymous? Individual accounts? Organizational (Azure AD)?

## Pick the Right Interactivity Level

Blazor render modes are a progression scale. Start at the simplest level that satisfies the requirements and only move up when there's a concrete reason.

```
Static SSR ──→ SSR + Enhanced Nav ──→ Interactive Server ──→ Interactive WebAssembly
 simplest                                                           most complex
```

### Decision Rules

| If the app needs... | Use | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Display data, simple forms, links between pages | **Static SSR** (`-int None`) | No JS runtime, no circuit, no WebAssembly download. Forms work via HTML POST. Enhanced navigation makes it feel snappy. |
| Everything above + a few components with client-side behavior (live search, real-time updates, complex form wizards) | **Interactive Server, per-page** (`-int Server`) | Only the components that need interactivity opt in with `@rendermode`. The rest stays static. Server-side execution, full .NET access, no API layer needed. |
| Most pages need rich interactivity (dashboards, drag-and-drop, chat) | **Interactive Server, global** (`-int Server -ai`) | Every component is interactive by default. Consistent UX, simpler mental model. Trade-off: every user holds a SignalR circuit on the server. |
| Network latency is a problem, users are on mobile/poor connections, or the app must work offline | **Interactive WebAssembly** (`-int WebAssembly`) | Code runs in the browser. Eliminates round-trip latency but requires a `.Client` project, API layer for data access, and downloads the .NET runtime to the browser on first visit. For offline support, enable PWA: add a service worker and manifest after scaffolding (not included in the template by default). |
| Fast initial load (Server) + low latency after (WebAssembly) | **Interactive Auto** (`-int Auto`) | First visit uses Server; subsequent visits use cached WebAssembly runtime. Most complex setup — see Auto constraints below. Only choose when both Server and WebAssembly constraints apply. |

**Default recommendation:** Start with `-int Server` (per-page). It covers the vast majority of apps. Upgrade to global or WebAssembly only when a specific requirement demands it.

### Auto Mode Constraints

Auto mode means your component code runs on the server first, then in the browser on subsequent visits. This creates real constraints:

- **All interactive components must live in the `.Client` project** — same as WebAssembly.
- **No direct server access** from interactive components — no EF `DbContext`, no file system, no server-only services. All data access must go through HTTP APIs.
- **Both `Program.cs` files must register matching services** — the server and client DI containers must both provide implementations for any service an interactive component injects.
- **Code must not assume its execution environment** — no `HttpContext` access, no browser-only APIs without `RendererInfo` guards.
- **Test in both modes** — a component that works on Server during development may break on WebAssembly in production (second visit). Test both paths.

### Don'ts

- Don't pick WebAssembly "because it's cool" — it adds a `.Client` project, forces API-mediated data access, and downloads ~10MB to the browser on first visit.
- Don't pick Auto unless you can articulate why Server alone and WebAssembly alone are both insufficient.
- Don't pick global interactivity for apps where most pages are read-only content — per-page keeps the static pages fast and reduces server memory.

## Scaffold the Project

### Static SSR Only (display data + simple forms)

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int None
```

No interactive runtime. Enhanced navigation enabled by default via `blazor.web.js`.

### Interactive Server, Per-Page (recommended default)

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int Server
```

Pages are static by default. Add `@rendermode InteractiveServer` to components that need interactivity.

### Interactive Server, Global

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int Server -ai
```

All pages interactive via `<Routes @rendermode="InteractiveServer" />` in `App.razor`.

### Interactive WebAssembly, Per-Page

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int WebAssembly
```

Creates `{AppName}` (server) and `{AppName}.Client` (WebAssembly) projects. Interactive components must live in `.Client`.

### Interactive WebAssembly, Global

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int WebAssembly -ai
```

### Interactive Auto, Per-Page

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int Auto
```

### Interactive Auto, Global

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int Auto -ai
```

### With Authentication

Append `-au Individual` to any command above:

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int Server -au Individual
```

`-au Individual` scaffolds ASP.NET Core Identity with SQLite (CLI) or SQL Server (Visual Studio). Identity pages are always static SSR — they do not use interactive render modes.

The `blazor` template only supports `-au Individual`. For organizational auth (Microsoft Entra ID, Azure AD B2C), scaffold with `-au Individual` first, then replace the Identity provider with `Microsoft.Identity.Web` / OIDC middleware and configure the tenant in `appsettings.json`.

## What the Template Creates

### Single project (Static SSR, Server)

```
{AppName}/
├── Components/
│   ├── App.razor              # Root component — sets <HeadOutlet> and <Routes>
│   ├── Routes.razor           # Wraps <Router> with route discovery
│   ├── Layout/
│   │   ├── MainLayout.razor   # App shell with nav, header, footer
```

| WebAssembly, per-page (`-int WebAssembly`) | `assets/agents-md/webassembly-per-page.md` |
| WebAssembly, global (`-int WebAssembly -ai`) | `assets/agents-md/webassembly-global.md` |
| Auto, per-page (`-int Auto`) | `assets/agents-md/auto-per-page.md` |
| Auto, global (`-int Auto -ai`) | `assets/agents-md/auto-global.md` |

---
license: MIT
name: create-blazor-project
description: >
  Create a new ASP.NET Core web application or web site using Blazor.
  USE FOR: creating a new Blazor web app, scaffolding a new web project,
  starting a new web site, choosing render modes (Static SSR, Interactive Server,
  Interactive WebAssembly, Auto), running dotnet new blazor with the right options,
  setting up initial project structure.
  DO NOT USE FOR: adding features to existing projects, changing how an existing
  app renders, or component authoring (use author-component).
---

# Create a Blazor Web App

## Before You Start — Gather Requirements

If the user's request doesn't make the following clear, ask before scaffolding:

1. **What does the app do?** List the main screens/features (e.g., "product catalog with search and shopping cart").
2. **What kind of interactivity is needed?** Displaying data and forms? Real-time updates? Offline support? Rich drag-and-drop UI?
3. **Deployment environment?** Internet-facing? Intranet? Mobile users on slow connections?
4. **Authentication needed?** Anonymous? Individual accounts? Organizational (Azure AD)?

## Pick the Right Interactivity Level

Blazor render modes are a progression scale. Start at the simplest level that satisfies the requirements and only move up when there's a concrete reason.

```
Static SSR ──→ SSR + Enhanced Nav ──→ Interactive Server ──→ Interactive WebAssembly
 simplest                                                           most complex
```

### Decision Rules

| If the app needs... | Use | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Display data, simple forms, links between pages | **Static SSR** (`-int None`) | No JS runtime, no circuit, no WebAssembly download. Forms work via HTML POST. Enhanced navigation makes it feel snappy. |
| Everything above + a few components with client-side behavior (live search, real-time updates, complex form wizards) | **Interactive Server, per-page** (`-int Server`) | Only the components that need interactivity opt in with `@rendermode`. The rest stays static. Server-side execution, full .NET access, no API layer needed. |
| Most pages need rich interactivity (dashboards, drag-and-drop, chat) | **Interactive Server, global** (`-int Server -ai`) | Every component is interactive by default. Consistent UX, simpler mental model. Trade-off: every user holds a SignalR circuit on the server. |
| Network latency is a problem, users are on mobile/poor connections, or the app must work offline | **Interactive WebAssembly** (`-int WebAssembly`) | Code runs in the browser. Eliminates round-trip latency but requires a `.Client` project, API layer for data access, and downloads the .NET runtime to the browser on first visit. For offline support, enable PWA: add a service worker and manifest after scaffolding (not included in the template by default). |
| Fast initial load (Server) + low latency after (WebAssembly) | **Interactive Auto** (`-int Auto`) | First visit uses Server; subsequent visits use cached WebAssembly runtime. Most complex setup — see Auto constraints below. Only choose when both Server and WebAssembly constraints apply. |

**Default recommendation:** Start with `-int Server` (per-page). It covers the vast majority of apps. Upgrade to global or WebAssembly only when a specific requirement demands it.

### Auto Mode Constraints

Auto mode means your component code runs on the server first, then in the browser on subsequent visits. This creates real constraints:

- **All interactive components must live in the `.Client` project** — same as WebAssembly.
- **No direct server access** from interactive components — no EF `DbContext`, no file system, no server-only services. All data access must go through HTTP APIs.
- **Both `Program.cs` files must register matching services** — the server and client DI containers must both provide implementations for any service an interactive component injects.
- **Code must not assume its execution environment** — no `HttpContext` access, no browser-only APIs without `RendererInfo` guards.
- **Test in both modes** — a component that works on Server during development may break on WebAssembly in production (second visit). Test both paths.

### Don'ts

- Don't pick WebAssembly "because it's cool" — it adds a `.Client` project, forces API-mediated data access, and downloads ~10MB to the browser on first visit.
- Don't pick Auto unless you can articulate why Server alone and WebAssembly alone are both insufficient.
- Don't pick global interactivity for apps where most pages are read-only content — per-page keeps the static pages fast and reduces server memory.

## Scaffold the Project

### Static SSR Only (display data + simple forms)

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int None
```

No interactive runtime. Enhanced navigation enabled by default via `blazor.web.js`.

### Interactive Server, Per-Page (recommended default)

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int Server
```

Pages are static by default. Add `@rendermode InteractiveServer` to components that need interactivity.

### Interactive Server, Global

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int Server -ai
```

All pages interactive via `<Routes @rendermode="InteractiveServer" />` in `App.razor`.

### Interactive WebAssembly, Per-Page

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int WebAssembly
```

Creates `{AppName}` (server) and `{AppName}.Client` (WebAssembly) projects. Interactive components must live in `.Client`.

### Interactive WebAssembly, Global

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int WebAssembly -ai
```

### Interactive Auto, Per-Page

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int Auto
```

### Interactive Auto, Global

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int Auto -ai
```

### With Authentication

Append `-au Individual` to any command above:

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int Server -au Individual
```

`-au Individual` scaffolds ASP.NET Core Identity with SQLite (CLI) or SQL Server (Visual Studio). Identity pages are always static SSR — they do not use interactive render modes.

The `blazor` template only supports `-au Individual`. For organizational auth (Microsoft Entra ID, Azure AD B2C), scaffold with `-au Individual` first, then replace the Identity provider with `Microsoft.Identity.Web` / OIDC middleware and configure the tenant in `appsettings.json`.

## What the Template Creates

### Single project (Static SSR, Server)

```
{AppName}/
├── Components/
│   ├── App.razor              # Root component — sets <HeadOutlet> and <Routes>
│   ├── Routes.razor           # Wraps <Router> with route discovery
│   ├── Layout/
│   │   ├── MainLayout.razor   # App shell with nav, header, footer
│   │   └── MainLayout.razor.css
│   └── Pages/
│       └── Home.razor         # @page "/" — first page
├── Program.cs                 # Service registration and middleware
├── wwwroot/                   # Static files (CSS, images)
└── {AppName}.csproj
```

### Two projects (WebAssembly, Auto)

```
{AppName}/                     # Server project — hosts the app
├── Components/                # Server-only components (static SSR pages, layouts)
│   ├── App.razor
│   ├── Routes.razor
│   └── Layout/
├── Program.cs                 # Server Program.cs
└── {AppName}.Client/          # Client project — WebAssembly components
    ├── Pages/                 # Interactive components go HERE
    ├── Program.cs             # Client Program.cs
    └── _Imports.razor
```

**Rule:** Components using `InteractiveWebAssembly` or `InteractiveAuto` must live in the `.Client` project. They can reference shared code but cannot reference server-only types (EF `DbContext`, server-side services).

## Program.cs Wiring

The template generates the correct `Program.cs` for the chosen mode. Verify these registrations match your intent:

### Static SSR Only

```csharp
// Program.cs
builder.Services.AddRazorComponents();

// ...

app.MapRazorComponents<App>();
```

### Server (per-page or global)

```csharp
builder.Services.AddRazorComponents()
    .AddInteractiveServerComponents();

// ...

app.MapRazorComponents<App>()
    .AddInteractiveServerRenderMode();
```

### WebAssembly (per-page or global)

```csharp
// Server Program.cs
builder.Services.AddRazorComponents()
    .AddInteractiveWebAssemblyComponents();

// ...

app.MapRazorComponents<App>()
    .AddInteractiveWebAssemblyRenderMode()
    .AddAdditionalAssemblies(typeof({AppName}.Client._Imports).Assembly);
```

```csharp
// Client Program.cs
builder.Services.AddAuthorizationCore();
// Register HttpClient, other client-side services
```

## Create Project AGENTS.md

After scaffolding, create an `AGENTS.md` file in the project root (next to the `.csproj`). For two-project setups, put it in the server project root.

Pick the matching template from `assets/agents-md/` based on the chosen mode:

| Mode | Template file |
|------|--------------|
| Static SSR (`-int None`) | `assets/agents-md/ssr-none.md` |
| Server, per-page (`-int Server`) | `assets/agents-md/server-per-page.md` |
| Server, global (`-int Server -ai`) | `assets/agents-md/server-global.md` |
| WebAssembly, per-page (`-int WebAssembly`) | `assets/agents-md/webassembly-per-page.md` |
| WebAssembly, global (`-int WebAssembly -ai`) | `assets/agents-md/webassembly-global.md` |
| Auto, per-page (`-int Auto`) | `assets/agents-md/auto-per-page.md` |
| Auto, global (`-int Auto -ai`) | `assets/agents-md/auto-global.md` |

Copy the template contents into the project's `AGENTS.md` and replace every `{AppName}` with the actual project name. If auth was scaffolded (`-au Individual`), add an `## Authentication` section noting that ASP.NET Core Identity is configured and that Identity pages under `Components/Account/` are always static SSR — do not add `@rendermode` to them.

**After scaffolding the project and creating AGENTS.md, continue implementing the features the user requested.** Remove default template pages (Counter, Weather) and replace them with the actual application pages.

### Auto (per-page or global)

```csharp
// Server Program.cs
builder.Services.AddRazorComponents()
    .AddInteractiveServerComponents()
    .AddInteractiveWebAssemblyComponents();

// ...

app.MapRazorComponents<App>()
    .AddInteractiveServerRenderMode()
    .AddInteractiveWebAssemblyRenderMode()
    .AddAdditionalAssemblies(typeof({AppName}.Client._Imports).Assembly);
```

## App.razor — Global vs Per-Page

The difference between global and per-page interactivity is entirely in `App.razor`:

### Per-page (default)

```razor
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
    <HeadOutlet />
</head>
<body>
    <Routes />
    <script src="_framework/blazor.web.js"></script>
</body>
</html>
```

No `@rendermode` on `<Routes>` or `<HeadOutlet>`. Individual pages opt in.

### Global

```razor
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
    <HeadOutlet @rendermode="InteractiveServer" />
</head>
<body>
    <Routes @rendermode="InteractiveServer" />
    <script src="_framework/blazor.web.js"></script>
</body>
</html>
```

Replace `InteractiveServer` with `InteractiveWebAssembly` or `InteractiveAuto` as appropriate.

## After Scaffolding

1. **Verify it builds:** `dotnet build`
2. **Run it:** `dotnet run` (in the server project if two-project setup)
3. **Add your first page:** Create a `.razor` file in `Components/Pages/` (server project) or `Pages/` (`.Client` project for WebAssembly components)

## Don'ts

- Don't use `dotnet new blazorwasm` — that creates a standalone WebAssembly SPA without server-side rendering. Use the `blazor` template with `-int WebAssembly` instead.
- Don't manually add `AddInteractiveServerComponents()` to a project created with `-int None` and expect it to work — you also need the `@rendermode` directives and potentially `App.razor` changes. Re-scaffold if the mode needs to change fundamentally.
- Don't put WebAssembly-targeted components in the server project — they'll work during prerender but fail after handoff.

data.reasoningText:
Based on the requirements:
- Offline support is critical (poor/no cellular connectivity)
- Low-latency UI (inspectors tap through items quickly)
- Mobile phones and tablets
- No authentication needed

This clearly calls for **Interactive WebAssembly** mode because:
1. Must work offline → WebAssembly runs in browser, can work without server connection
2. Low latency → no server round-trips
3. Mobile with poor connectivity → can't rely on SignalR circuits (Server mode would break)

I'll use global WebAssembly since most/all pages need interactivity (checklist tapping, photo capture, offline data entry).


Now I need to check the webassembly-global.md template to understand the project structure, then set up the scaffolding accordingly.

arguments.name: implement-inspection-app
arguments.prompt:
You are implementing a Blazor WebAssembly app for building inspectors on construction sites. The project is already scaffolded at `/tmp/sv-71376a3ad16945aaa955dfb4331c2d4c/SiteInspector/`.

Structure:
- `SiteInspector/` - Server project (hosts app, API endpoints)
- `SiteInspector.Client/` - WebAssembly project (all interactive UI components)

## Requirements
1. Inspectors fill out safety checklists and add notes at each site visit
2. Photos are attached to inspection reports
3. Must work offline (poor/no cellular connectivity on construction sites)
4. When connectivity returns, data syncs back to the server
5. Mobile-first (phones and tablets)
6. Low-latency UI — inspectors tap through checklist items quickly
7. No authentication needed

## Architecture Plan

### Offline-First with IndexedDB
- Use browser's IndexedDB (via JS interop) to store all inspection data locally
- A background sync service detects connectivity and pushes data to server APIs
- PWA with service worker for caching app shell and static assets

### Data Model
- **Site**: Id, Name, Address
- **Inspection**: Id, SiteId, InspectorName, StartedAt, CompletedAt, Notes, Status (InProgress/Completed/Synced)
- **ChecklistItem**: Id, InspectionId, Category (e.g., "Fall Protection", "Electrical", "Scaffolding", "PPE", "Housekeeping"), Description, IsCompliant (Yes/No/NA), Notes
- **Photo**: Id, InspectionId, ChecklistItemId (nullable), FileName, Base64Data, CapturedAt

### Pages (in SiteInspector.Client/Pages/)
1. **Home.razor** - List of sites, quick-start new inspection
2. **NewInspection.razor** - Select site, enter inspector name, start inspection
3. **Checklist.razor** - The main checklist screen with categories, tap to mark compliant/non-compliant/NA, add notes per item
4. **PhotoCapture.razor** - Camera capture (use HTML input type=file with accept="image/*" and capture attribute for mobile)
5. **InspectionSummary.razor** - Review completed inspection, view all items and photos
6. **SyncStatus.razor** - Shows sync queue, connectivity status, manual sync trigger

### Implementation Details

#### 1. Remove template default pages
Delete Counter.razor and Weather.razor from `SiteInspector.Client/Pages/`. Delete any Weather-related files from the server project.

#### 2. Create models in `SiteInspector.Client/Models/`
Create C# record/class files for Site, Inspection, ChecklistItem, Photo, and a ChecklistTemplate (predefined items).

#### 3. Create `SiteInspector.Client/Services/`
- `IInspectionStore.cs` - Interface for local data persistence
- `IndexedDbStore.cs` - Implementation using JS interop to IndexedDB
- `ISyncService.cs` - Interface for sync operations  
- `SyncService.cs` - Background sync implementation
- `ConnectivityService.cs` - Monitors online/offline state

#### 4. JavaScript interop file: `SiteInspector.Client/wwwroot/js/indexedDb.js`
Implement IndexedDB operations: openDb, addRecord, getRecord, getAllRecords, updateRecord, deleteRecord, getRecordsByIndex.

#### 5. PWA Support
- Add `manifest.json` to `SiteInspector/wwwroot/` with app name "Site Inspector", theme color, icons
- Add `service-worker.js` to `SiteInspector/wwwroot/` that caches the app shell
- Add manifest link and service worker registration to `App.razor`

#### 6. Mobile-first CSS
- Create `SiteInspector.Client/wwwroot/css/app.css` with large touch targets (min 48px), swipe-friendly layout
- Use viewport meta tag (already present)

#### 7. Server API endpoints (in SiteInspector/Program.cs)
Add minimal API endpoints:
- `POST /api/inspections/sync` - Receives inspection data batch
- `GET /api/sites` - Returns list of sites
- `GET /api/inspections` - Returns synced inspections

#### 8. Server-side storage
For simplicity, use an in-memory store on the server (can be replaced with a database later). Create `SiteInspector/Services/InspectionRepository.cs`.

### Checklist Template
Pre-populate with standard construction safety checklist categories:
- **Fall Protection**: Guardrails in place, Safety nets installed, Harnesses available, Floor openings covered
- **Scaffolding**: Base plates secured, Planking in good condition, Guardrails installed, Access ladders provided
- **Electrical**: GFCI protection in use, Cords in good condition, Panels accessible, Lockout/tagout procedures
- **PPE**: Hard hats worn, Safety glasses worn, High-vis vests worn, Proper footwear
- **Housekeeping**: Walkways clear, Materials stored properly, Waste containers available, No tripping hazards
- **Fire Safety**: Extinguishers accessible, Hot work permits posted, Flammable storage proper, Emergency exits clear

### Key Implementation Notes
- The checklist screen should be FAST — large tap targets for Yes/No/NA buttons, swipe between categories
- Photos use `<input type="file" accept="image/*" capture="environment">` for mobile camera access
- Store photos as base64 in IndexedDB (keeps it simple for offline)
- Show a connectivity indicator in the nav/header
- The sync service should use a timer to periodically attempt sync when online
- Register all services in `SiteInspector.Client/Program.cs`
- Use `NavigationManager` for page navigation

### File Changes Summary
Files to DELETE:
- `SiteInspector.Client/Pages/Counter.razor`
- `SiteInspector.Client/Pages/Home.razor` (will recreate)
- `SiteInspector.Client/Pages/Weather.razor` (if exists)
- `SiteInspector/Components/Pages/` (server-side pages not needed)

Files to CREATE in `SiteInspector.Client/`:
- `Models/Site.cs`
- `Models/Inspection.cs`
- `Models/ChecklistItem.cs`
- `Models/Photo.cs`
- `Models/ChecklistTemplate.cs`
- `Models/SyncStatus.cs`
- `Services/IInspectionStore.cs`
- `Services/IndexedDbStore.cs`
- `Services/ISyncService.cs`
- `Services/SyncService.cs`
- `Services/ConnectivityService.cs`
- `Pages/Home.razor`
- `Pages/NewInspection.razor`
- `Pages/Checklist.razor`
- `Pages/PhotoCapture.razor`
- `Pages/InspectionSummary.razor`
- `Pages/SyncStatusPage.razor`
- `wwwroot/js/indexedDb.js`
- `wwwroot/css/app.css`

Files to CREATE in `SiteInspector/`:
- `Services/InspectionRepository.cs`
- `wwwroot/manifest.json`
- `wwwroot/service-worker.js`
- `wwwroot/icons/icon-192.png` (placeholder - create a simple SVG instead: `wwwroot/icons/icon.svg`)

Files to MODIFY:
- `SiteInspector/Program.cs` - Add API endpoints and server services
- `SiteInspector.Client/Program.cs` - Register client services, HttpClient
- `SiteInspector/Components/App.razor` - Add manifest link, service worker script, CSS link
- `SiteInspector.Client/_Imports.razor` - Add using statements for Models and Services
- `SiteInspector.Client/Layout/MainLayout.razor` - Mobile-friendly layout with connectivity indicator

Please implement ALL of this. Make sure the project builds successfully by running `dotnet build` at the end from the solution directory `/tmp/sv-71376a3ad16945aaa955dfb4331c2d4c/SiteInspector/`.

+namespace SiteInspector.Client.Models;
+
+public static class ChecklistTemplate
+{
+    public static readonly Dictionary<string, string[]> Categories = new()
+    {
+        ["Fall Protection"] = new[]
+        {
+            "Guardrails in place",
+            "Safety nets installed",
+            "Harnesses available",
+            "Floor openings covered"
+        },
+        ["Scaffolding"] = new[]
+        {
+            "Base plates secured",
+            "Planking in good condition",
+            "Guardrails installed",
+            "Access ladders provided"
+        },
+        ["Electrical"] = new[]
+        {
+            "GFCI protection in use",
+            "Cords in good condition",
+            "Panels accessible",
+            "Lockout/tagout procedures"
+        },
+        ["PPE"] = new[]
+        {
+            "Hard hats worn",
+            "Safety glasses worn",
+            "High-vis vests worn",
+            "Proper footwear"
+        },
+        ["Housekeeping"] = new[]
+        {
+            "Walkways clear",
+            "Materials stored properly",
+            "Waste containers available",
+            "No tripping hazards"
+        },
+        ["Fire Safety"] = new[]
+        {
+            "Extinguishers accessible",
+            "Hot work permits posted",
+            "Flammable storage proper",
+            "Emergency exits clear"
+        }
+    };
+
+    public static List<ChecklistItem> CreateItemsForInspection(string inspectionId)
+    {
+        var items = new List<ChecklistItem>();
+        foreach (var (category, descriptions) in Categories)
+        {
+            foreach (var description in descriptions)
+            {
+                items.Add(new ChecklistItem
+                {
+                    InspectionId = inspectionId,
+                    Category = category,
+                    Description = description
+                });
+            }
+        }
+        return items;
+    }
+}
+

data.content:
Build succeeds. Here's what was implemented:

**Site Inspector PWA** — an offline-first Blazor WebAssembly app for construction safety inspections:

- **Models**: Site, Inspection, ChecklistItem, Photo, SyncStatusInfo, ChecklistTemplate (6 categories, 24 items)
- **Services**: IndexedDB store (JS interop), ConnectivityService (online/offline detection), SyncService (background timer-based sync)
- **Pages**: Home (site list), NewInspection, Checklist (category tabs, tap Yes/No/NA), PhotoCapture (mobile camera), InspectionSummary, SyncStatusPage
- **PWA**: manifest.json, service-worker.js, app shell caching
- **Server**: Minimal API endpoints (`POST /api/inspections/sync`, `GET /api/sites`, `GET /api/inspections`), in-memory repository
- **Mobile-first CSS**: 48px touch targets, sticky header with connectivity indicator, bottom nav, swipe-friendly category tabs

You are implementing a Blazor WebAssembly app for building inspectors on construction sites. The project is already scaffolded at `/tmp/sv-71376a3ad16945aaa955dfb4331c2d4c/SiteInspector/`.

Structure:
- `SiteInspector/` - Server project (hosts app, API endpoints)
- `SiteInspector.Client/` - WebAssembly project (all interactive UI components)

## Requirements
1. Inspectors fill out safety checklists and add notes at each site visit
2. Photos are attached to inspection reports
3. Must work offline (poor/no cellular connectivity on construction sites)
4. When connectivity returns, data syncs back to the server
5. Mobile-first (phones and tablets)
6. Low-latency UI — inspectors tap through checklist items quickly
7. No authentication needed

## Architecture Plan

### Offline-First with IndexedDB
- Use browser's IndexedDB (via JS interop) to store all inspection data locally
- A background sync service detects connectivity and pushes data to server APIs
- PWA with service worker for caching app shell and static assets

### Data Model
- **Site**: Id, Name, Address
- **Inspection**: Id, SiteId, InspectorName, StartedAt, CompletedAt, Notes, Status (InProgress/Completed/Synced)
- **ChecklistItem**: Id, InspectionId, Category (e.g., "Fall Protection", "Electrical", "Scaffolding", "PPE", "Housekeeping"), Description, IsCompliant (Yes/No/NA), Notes
- **Photo**: Id, InspectionId, ChecklistItemId (nullable), FileName, Base64Data, CapturedAt

### Pages (in SiteInspector.Client/Pages/)
1. **Home.razor** - List of sites, quick-start new inspection
2. **NewInspection.razor** - Select site, enter inspector name, start inspection
3. **Checklist.razor** - The main checklist screen with categories, tap to mark compliant/non-compliant/NA, add notes per item
4. **PhotoCapture.razor** - Camera capture (use HTML input type=file with accept="image/*" and capture attribute for mobile)
5. **InspectionSummary.razor** - Review completed inspection, view all items and photos
6. **SyncStatus.razor** - Shows sync queue, connectivity status, manual sync trigger

### Implementation Details

#### 1. Remove template default pages
Delete Counter.razor and Weather.razor from `SiteInspector.Client/Pages/`. Delete any Weather-related files from the server project.

#### 2. Create models in `SiteInspector.Client/Models/`
Create C# record/class files for Site, Inspection, ChecklistItem, Photo, and a ChecklistTemplate (predefined items).

#### 3. Create `SiteInspector.Client/Services/`
- `IInspectionStore.cs` - Interface for local data persistence
- `IndexedDbStore.cs` - Implementation using JS interop to IndexedDB
- `ISyncService.cs` - Interface for sync operations  
- `SyncService.cs` - Background sync implementation
- `ConnectivityService.cs` - Monitors online/offline state

#### 4. JavaScript interop file: `SiteInspector.Client/wwwroot/js/indexedDb.js`
Implement IndexedDB operations: openDb, addRecord, getRecord, getAllRecords, updateRecord, deleteRecord, getRecordsByIndex.

#### 5. PWA Support
- Add `manifest.json` to `SiteInspector/wwwroot/` with app name "Site Inspector", theme color, icons
- Add `service-worker.js` to `SiteInspector/wwwroot/` that caches the app shell
- Add manifest link and service worker registration to `App.razor`

#### 6. Mobile-first CSS
- Create `SiteInspector.Client/wwwroot/css/app.css` with large touch targets (min 48px), swipe-friendly layout
- Use viewport meta tag (already present)

#### 7. Server API endpoints (in SiteInspector/Program.cs)
Add minimal API endpoints:
- `POST /api/inspections/sync` - Receives inspection data batch
- `GET /api/sites` - Returns list of sites
- `GET /api/inspections` - Returns synced inspections

#### 8. Server-side storage
For simplicity, use an in-memory store on the server (can be replaced with a database later). Create `SiteInspector/Services/InspectionRepository.cs`.

### Checklist Template
Pre-populate with standard construction safety checklist categories:
- **Fall Protection**: Guardrails in place, Safety nets installed, Harnesses available, Floor openings covered
- **Scaffolding**: Base plates secured, Planking in good condition, Guardrails installed, Access ladders provided
- **Electrical**: GFCI protection in use, Cords in good condition, Panels accessible, Lockout/tagout procedures
- **PPE**: Hard hats worn, Safety glasses worn, High-vis vests worn, Proper footwear
- **Housekeeping**: Walkways clear, Materials stored properly, Waste containers available, No tripping hazards
- **Fire Safety**: Extinguishers accessible, Hot work permits posted, Flammable storage proper, Emergency exits clear

### Key Implementation Notes
- The checklist screen should be FAST — large tap targets for Yes/No/NA buttons, swipe between categories
- Photos use `<input type="file" accept="image/*" capture="environment">` for mobile camera access
- Store photos as base64 in IndexedDB (keeps it simple for offline)
- Show a connectivity indicator in the nav/header
- The sync service should use a timer to periodically attempt sync when online
- Register all services in `SiteInspector.Client/Program.cs`
- Use `NavigationManager` for page navigation

### File Changes Summary
Files to DELETE:
- `SiteInspector.Client/Pages/Counter.razor`
- `SiteInspector.Client/Pages/Home.razor` (will recreate)
- `SiteInspector.Client/Pages/Weather.razor` (if exists)
- `SiteInspector/Components/Pages/` (server-side pages not needed)

Files to CREATE in `SiteInspector.Client/`:
- `Models/Site.cs`
- `Models/Inspection.cs`
- `Models/ChecklistItem.cs`
- `Models/Photo.cs`
- `Models/ChecklistTemplate.cs`
- `Models/SyncStatus.cs`
- `Services/IInspectionStore.cs`
- `Services/IndexedDbStore.cs`
- `Services/ISyncService.cs`
- `Services/SyncService.cs`
- `Services/ConnectivityService.cs`
- `Pages/Home.razor`

- `Pages/PhotoCapture.razor`
- `Pages/InspectionSummary.razor`
- `Pages/SyncStatusPage.razor`
- `wwwroot/js/indexedDb.js`
- `wwwroot/css/app.css`

Files to CREATE in `SiteInspector/`:
- `Services/InspectionRepository.cs`
- `wwwroot/manifest.json`
- `wwwroot/service-worker.js`
- `wwwroot/icons/icon-192.png` (placeholder - create a simple SVG instead: `wwwroot/icons/icon.svg`)

Files to MODIFY:
- `SiteInspector/Program.cs` - Add API endpoints and server services
- `SiteInspector.Client/Program.cs` - Register client services, HttpClient
- `SiteInspector/Components/App.razor` - Add manifest link, service worker script, CSS link
- `SiteInspector.Client/_Imports.razor` - Add using statements for Models and Services
- `SiteInspector.Client/Layout/MainLayout.razor` - Mobile-friendly layout with connectivity indicator

# SiteInspector

| Setting | Value |
|---------|-------|
| **Interactivity Mode** | WebAssembly |
| **Interactivity Scope** | Global |

## Rendering configuration
This project uses global Interactive WebAssembly with prerendering.
Created with `dotnet new blazor -int WebAssembly -ai`.

All pages are interactive by default via `<Routes @rendermode="InteractiveWebAssembly" />` in `App.razor`. Components run entirely in the browser after the initial prerender.

## Project structure
- **SiteInspector** (server): Hosts the Blazor app, serves static files, API endpoints.
- **SiteInspector.Client** (WebAssembly): All interactive UI components. Runs entirely in the browser.

## Adding new components
- All interactive components MUST go in the `.Client` project, not the server project.
- New pages go in `SiteInspector.Client/Pages/`.
- All pages are already interactive. No need to add `@rendermode` to individual components.

## Data access
Interactive components cannot access the database directly. Use this pattern:
1. Define an interface in the `.Client` project (e.g., `IDataService`).
2. In the `.Client` project, implement it using `HttpClient` to call server APIs.
3. In the server project, implement it using direct data access (EF Core DbContext, etc.).
4. Register the client implementation in the client `Program.cs` and the server implementation in the server `Program.cs`.
5. Expose server data through minimal API endpoints (e.g., `app.MapGet(...)`) that the client implementation calls.

## Offline architecture
- All inspection data is stored locally in IndexedDB via JS interop (`wwwroot/js/indexedDb.js`).
- `ConnectivityService` monitors online/offline state via browser events.
- `SyncService` runs a background timer that pushes local data to server APIs when online.
- The app is a PWA with service worker (`wwwroot/service-worker.js`) and manifest (`wwwroot/manifest.json`).

## Service registration
- Client-side services go in `SiteInspector.Client/Program.cs`.
- Server-side services go in `SiteInspector/Program.cs`.

## Environment constraints
- Components run in the browser via WebAssembly. No `HttpContext`, no server file system.
- All data access goes through `HttpClient` calls to server API endpoints.
- The .NET runtime (~10 MB) is downloaded to the browser on first visit and cached.

## Don'ts
- Don't put interactive components in the server project — they work during prerender but fail after WebAssembly handoff.
- Don't inject `DbContext` or server-only services in `.Client` project components — use HTTP APIs instead.
- Don't add `@rendermode InteractiveWebAssembly` to pages — global interactivity is already configured in `App.razor`.

The app is built and compiles cleanly. Here's what you have:

## Site Inspector — Offline-First Blazor PWA

**Architecture:** Interactive WebAssembly (global) — all UI runs in-browser, no server dependency for core functionality.

**Pages:**
| Route | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| `/` | Site list + start new inspection |
| `/new-inspection` | Select site, name inspector |
| `/checklist/{id}` | Tap through Yes/No/NA per item, categorized tabs |
| `/photo-capture/{id}` | Mobile camera capture, attach to inspection |
| `/inspection/{id}` | Summary review of completed inspection |
| `/sync` | Sync queue status, manual trigger |

**Offline strategy:**
- IndexedDB stores all data locally (inspections, checklist answers, photos as base64)
- Service worker caches the app shell for instant offline launch
- `SyncService` auto-pushes to server when connectivity returns

**Mobile UX:** 48px touch targets, sticky header with connectivity indicator, bottom navigation, category tabs for quick checklist traversal.

**To run:** `cd SiteInspector/SiteInspector && dotnet run`
